metadata.title: Who was Albert Einstein?
metadata.color_semantics: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 terracotta on quantum moments; SLATE #8A8678 for classical/muted elements
metadata.greeting_note: German hello — Einstein was born in Ulm, Germany. Arc: second in quantum-founders arc after 'Who was Max Planck?'
metadata.note: COLD OPEN LAW: B00 = ClaudeComposerAsk. ILLUSTRATE LAW: UI only at B00, B03, B06, B08, B09. ASK→RESULT LAW: B03=ask, B04=Manim result. HANDOFF LAW: B08='Your turn.' OUTRO LAW: B09=ClaudeTitleOutro restates title with ?. Spine: B00 cold open · B01 the problem · B02 the gambit (Manim E=hf) · B03 ask · B04 result (Manim E=mc²) · B05 reluctant founder · B06 legacy · B07 the man · B08 handoff · B09 outro.
metadata.description_blurb: Albert Einstein didn't just extend Planck's quantum theory — he took it seriously when nobody else dared. In 1905, alone in a Bern patent office, he sent five papers that rewrote physics. Light is a particle. Mass is frozen energy. Space and time bend. The man who built the door couldn't accept where it led.

beats.narration_text: Thanks Bear, let's recap with Claude. The photoelectric effect turns on frequency, not brightness, a fact classical wave theory could not explain. Einstein proposed real light packets, photons of energy E equals h times f, and the experiment matched exactly. Three months later special relativity kept the speed of light constant and produced E equals m c squared. He won the 1921 Nobel for the photoelectric effect, then spent thirty years fighting quantum randomness and lost.

props.artifactTitle: Einstein - the photon and its doubter
artifactLines: The photoelectric effect turns on frequency, not brightness, a fact classical wave theory could not explain.
Einstein proposed real light packets, photons of energy E equals h times f, and the experiment matched exactly.
Three months later special relativity kept the speed of light constant and produced E equals m c squared.
He won the 1921 Nobel for the photoelectric effect, then spent thirty years fighting quantum randomness and lost.

beats.narration_text: The irony you ended on grips me: the man who made photons real spent thirty years fighting the randomness quantum mechanics demanded, and lost. I want to understand the actual disagreement, not just the slogan. Help me: (1) state precisely what the Copenhagen interpretation claimed was fundamentally random, (2) explain what Einstein proposed instead and one concrete objection he raised against it, and (3) show which experiment or result ultimately decided the question against him, and why his 1921 Nobel came for the photoelectric effect rather than relativity.

props.command: The irony you ended on grips me: the man who made photons real spent thirty years fighting the randomness quantum mechanics demanded, and lost. I want to understand the actual disagreement, not just the slogan. Help me: (1) state precisely what the Copenhagen interpretation claimed was fundamentally random, (2) explain what Einstein proposed instead and one concrete objection he raised against it, and (3) show which experiment or result ultimately decided the question against him, and why his 1921 Nobel came for the photoelectric effect rather than relativity.

beats.narration_text: Physicists had data they couldn't explain. Shine light on metal and electrons come out — but only if the light's frequency is high enough. Brighter light doesn't help. Only higher frequency does. Classical wave theory said that made no sense. Something was wrong with classical physics.

card.copy: Why does light knock electrons out of metal — but only above a certain frequency?

beats.narration_text: Planck had quantized energy as a fix. Einstein made it real. Light itself comes in packets — photons. Each one carries energy E equals h times f: Planck's constant times frequency. Below the threshold frequency, photons don't have enough energy to eject an electron, no matter how many arrive. Above it, one photon kicks out one electron. The experiment matched exactly.

production_viz.label: E=hf equation on cream canvas; frequency-threshold ladder (terracotta above threshold, slate below); electron ejection arrow in terracotta

props.command: plot E=hf threshold effect — photon energy vs frequency with ejection threshold for the photoelectric effect

beats.narration_text: Three months after the photoelectric paper, Einstein published special relativity. Space and time are not fixed — they bend for different observers. But one thing is constant: the speed of light. Follow the algebra and you get E equals m c squared. Mass and energy are the same substance. c squared is around ninety quadrillion. A tiny mass is a staggering amount of energy. He called it a consequence. History called it the equation.

production_viz.label: E=mc² equation centered on cream canvas; bar chart comparing 1 kg mass (slate, small) vs 9×10^16 J energy (terracotta, tall); ×c² arrow between bars

beats.narration_text: Einstein won the Nobel Prize in 1921 — for the photoelectric effect, not relativity. The committee thought relativity was too speculative. But by then he was already at war with quantum mechanics. Bohr's Copenhagen interpretation said the universe was fundamentally random. Einstein spent thirty years trying to prove it wasn't. He was wrong. The man who gave photons to physics couldn't accept what physics had become.

card.copy: "God does not play dice with the universe."

beats.spark_line: The door he couldn't walk through.

beats.narration_text: From one year — 1905 — an entire century follows: atomic physics, nuclear energy, GPS, the laser, the transistor, quantum cryptography. And in 1915, ten years after the miracle year, general relativity: gravity is curved spacetime. Black holes. The expanding universe. It all traces to a patent clerk who decided Planck's formula was telling the truth.

artifactLines: March 1905 — Photoelectric effect: light as photon quanta (Nobel Prize 1921)
May 1905 — Brownian motion: proof that atoms are real
June 1905 — Special relativity: space and time are relative; c is constant
1915 — General relativity: gravity is curved spacetime
1935 — EPR paradox: Einstein's challenge to quantum completeness

beats.narration_text: Einstein was born in Ulm in 1879. He left Germany in 1933 when Hitler came to power and never went back. Princeton gave him a desk. He spent his last twenty years on a unified field theory — trying to bring gravity and electromagnetism into one equation. He never finished it. He died in 1955 and asked to be cremated — no grave, no monument. His brain was removed without consent and studied for decades. Even in death, he was an experiment.

card.copy: Albert Einstein, 1879–1955. Nobel Prize 1921. Left Germany 1933. Princeton Institute for Advanced Study. American citizen 1940. Unified field theory — unfinished.

beats.narration_text: Want to go deeper? Take this prompt and paste it into Claude.

props.command: Explain how Einstein's 1905 photoelectric paper used Planck's constant to prove photons exist. Keep it short and concrete.
